summary:
Ai Bunar is a prehistoric copper mine located near Stara Zagora in central Bulgaria, representing one of the earliest known systematic metal mining operations in the world. Dated to approximately 5000–4500 BCE, Ai Bunar consists of eleven excavated trenches following surface outcrops of malachite and azurite — copper carbonate minerals — and provides physical evidence for the [[event:1567|earliest copper mining in the Balkans]]. The site takes its Turkish name (meaning 'copper spring' or 'copper source') from the characteristic green color of its ore deposits.

Excavations at Ai Bunar revealed mining tools — antler picks, stone hammers, and wooden wedges — in the filled trenches, confirming prehistoric working of the deposits. Chemical trace-element analysis of copper artifacts from Balkan Chalcolithic sites including the [[event:1559|Varna Necropolis]] has matched their composition to Ai Bunar ore sources, demonstrating that copper mined here was distributed across a wide area of southeastern Europe through established exchange networks. The correlation between mine source and artifact chemistry provides a rare direct link between extraction site and finished objects.

The Ai Bunar mine demonstrates that Chalcolithic communities of southeastern Europe had developed the knowledge to identify economically significant ore deposits, organize labor for their systematic extraction, and integrate metal production into regional exchange systems — capabilities that would underpin the more intensive Bronze Age metallurgy of subsequent millennia.
